title: practical english usage - Part 322

say, for example, Come for a climb with us or I'm going for a read.

This structure is only used with certain action-nouns - we  would probably not I'm going for a shower. Can you answer my phone if it rings?

Would you like to come for a drink this evening?

We need some fresh air. Let's go for a walk.

go for a bath, a shower go/come for a walk, a run, a swim, a ride, a drive, a drink, a meal examples: casual and probably rather short. (Compare go .. .ing, ►    476.) Common actions, mostly leisure activities. He seemed to be in a bad temper, but I didn't give it a thought.

'Are you coming to the film? 'No, I'm tired. I'll give it a miss'. (BrE) 'Perhaps salt will make it taste better.' 'OK, let's give it a try.' If the car won't start, we'll give it a push.

She gave me a strange look. I'll give you a call if I hear anything.

give it a miss (BrE)   not give it a thought give something a push, a kick give it a try, a go, a shot give somebody a smile, a look, a kiss, a hug,  a call Common expressions: American English) to replace transitive verbs, especially in an informal style.

The  structure is also used with an indirect object (in both British English and 2  give     somebody           a  smile,      etc Suddenly she gave a loud scream and fell to the ground.

He gave a cough to attract my attention.

happens in British English, for example, with verbs referring to sounds made by We   can replace certain verbs by a structure with give and a noun. When I came back my car was gone.  Is the butter all gone?

You can go out shopping but don't be gone too long.

She's been gone for three hours - what do you think she's doing?

something has disappeared or that there is no more.

Gone can be used like an adjective after be, to say that somebody is away, or that 477 gone      with    be   _____________________ t She went shopping at Harrods. (NOT   . . .  o Harrods.) I went swimming in the river. (NOT I went swimming to the river..) Note that prepositions of place, not direction, are used after go/come .. .ing.

Come swimming with us tomorrow.

come and go, ►    424).

Come.. .ing is also possible in certain situations (for the difference between 2 come      .. ring not beginning and end (     go boxing, go watching a football match).

We   do not use go .. .ing to talk about activities that have a more definite Anne's going fruit-picking this weekend.

In June all the students go looking for jobs.

I think I'll go shopping tomorrow.

Go.. .ing is also used to talk about looking for or collecting things.

Let's go climbing next weekend. Did you go dancing last Saturday?

go skiing, go swimming, go walking.

go dancing, go fishing, go hunting, go riding, go sailing, go shooting, go skating, expressions referring to sport and leisure activities - for example go climbing, and which  do not have a fixed beginning or end.
